content.problemStatement:
Let f(n)f(n)f(n) be the number of ways to partition the array [1,2,3,‚Ä¶,n][1, 2, 3, \ldots, n][1,2,3,‚Ä¶,n] into contiguous subarrays such that every pair of adjacent subarrays in the partition have sums of different parity.


    What is a contiguous subarray?
    A contiguous subarray of an array $A$ is an array that can be obtained by deleting some (possibly none) elements from the front of $A$ and some (possibly none) elements from the end of $A$. The subarrays of an array $A$ ($1$-indexed) of size $n$ are given by $[A_{i}, A_{i+1}, \ldots, A_j]$ for each pair of integers $(i, j)$ such that $1\leq i \leq j \leq n$.

    What is a partition of an array into contiguous subarrays?
    A partition of the array $A$ into contiguous subarrays is a set of subarrays of $A$, $\{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_k\}$, such that every element of the array belongs to exactly one of the subarrays $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$.

    Which partitions are counted in $f(n)$?
    Consider a partition $\{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k\}$ of the array $[1, 2, \ldots, n]$, where $S_i$ are in sorted order, i.e. every element in $S_i$ is smaller than every element in $S_{j}$ for all $i\lt j$. Then $f(n)$ is the number of partitions such that $\texttt{sum}(S_{i}) \not\equiv \texttt{sum}(S_{i+1}) \pmod{2} $ for all $1 \leq i \lt k$. $\texttt{sum}(S_{i})$ is the sum of all elements in $S_i$.
Let S0(n)=f(n)S_0(n) = f(n)S0‚Äã(n)=f(n) and Sk+1(n)=Sk(1)+Sk(2)+Sk(3)+‚ãØ+Sk(n)S_{k+1}(n) = S_k(1) + S_k(2) + S_k(3) + \cdots + S_k(n)Sk+1‚Äã(n)=Sk‚Äã(1)+Sk‚Äã(2)+Sk‚Äã(3)+‚ãØ+Sk‚Äã(n) for k‚â•0k \geq 0k‚â•0.

Given nnn and kkk, find Sk(n)‚Äämod‚Ää998 244 353S_k(n) \bmod 998\ 244\ 353Sk‚Äã(n)mod998 244 353.
constraints: The sum of kkk over all test cases does not exceed 300030003000.

k‚â§100k \leq 100k‚â§100 and is same for all test cases.

**Subtask #6 (40 points):**

`ExplanationWe first find f(n)f(n)f(n) for n=1,2,3,4,5n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5n=1,2,3,4,5.

When n=1n = 1n=1, there is only one partition [1][1][1]. Since there is only one subarray, there are no adjacent subarrays in this partition and hence should be counted in f(1)f(1)f(1), therefore f(1)=1f(1) = 1f(1)=1.
When n=2n = 2n=2, there are 222 partitions possible, [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [1],[2][1], [2][1],[2]. The first partition has no adjacent subarrays and so is counted in f(2)f(2)f(2).
The second partition has sums 1,21, 21,2 which are alternating in parity and so is also counted in f(2)f(2)f(2), therefore f(2)=2f(2) = 2f(2)=2.
When n=3n = 3n=3, there are 444 partitions possible. The partitions are shown below.

When n=4n = 4n=4, there are 888 partitions. The partitions are shown below.

PartitionSum of subarraysAre the sum of subarrays alternating in parity ?[1, 2, 3, 4]{10}Yes[1], [2, 3, 4]{1, 9}No[1, 2], [3, 4]{3, 7}No[1], [2], [3, 4]{1, 2, 7}Yes[1, 2, 3], [4]{6, 4}No[1], [2, 3], [4]{1, 5, 4}No[1, 2], [3], [4]{3, 3, 4}No[1], [2], [3], [4]{1, 2, 3, 4}YesThere are in total 222 partitions whose subarrays sums are alternating in parity, therefore f(3)=2f(3) = 2f(3)=2
When n=5n = 5n=5, there are in total 161616 partitions. The partitions are shown below.
S0(n)=f(n)S_0(n) = f(n)S0‚Äã(n)=f(n) and is found as described above.

To find S1(n),S2(n),‚Ä¶S_1(n), S_2(n), \ldotsS1‚Äã(n),S2‚Äã(n),‚Ä¶, the given formula Sk+1(n)=Sk(1)+Sk(2)+‚ãØ+Sk(n)S_{k+1}(n) = S_k(1) + S_k(2) + \cdots + S_k(n)Sk+1‚Äã(n)=Sk‚Äã(1)+Sk‚Äã(2)+‚ãØ+Sk‚Äã(n) is used.
For example S1(n)=S0(1)+S0(2)+‚ãØ+S0(n)S_1(n) = S_0(1) + S_0(2) + \cdots + S_0(n)S1‚Äã(n)=S0‚Äã(1)+S0‚Äã(2)+‚ãØ+S0‚Äã(n).
